content.claim: Long-run Hungarian evidence shows rural school-network size has swung repeatedly across political regimes: communist-era centralization (1950s-1970s) reduced primary schools from 6,000+ to 4,200 in 10 years; church schools were reduced from 4,100+ to ten after nationalization.
content.context: Fresh live research through the Political feasibility lens
limitations: Historical pattern does not predict current policy trajectory
Different regime contexts make direct comparison to present uncertain
No mechanism specified for how regime change would reverse current centralization
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
